sn22.5:1.2: on one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
sn22.5:1.3: There he addressed the monks: “Monks!” “Yes, lord,” the monks responded to him. The Blessed One said:
sn22.5:1.5: A concentrated monk discerns in line with what has come into being.
sn22.5:1.6: And what does he discern in line with what has come into being?
sn22.5:1.7: The origination & disappearance of form. The origination & disappearance of feeling… perception… fabrications. The origination & disappearance of consciousness.
sn22.5:2.1: And what is the origination of form… feeling… perception… fabrications? What is the origination of consciousness?
sn22.5:2.2: There is the case where one enjoys, welcomes, & remains fastened.
sn22.5:3.1: And what does one enjoy & welcome, to what does one remain fastened?
sn22.5:3.2: One enjoys, welcomes, & remains fastened to form.
sn22.5:3.3: As one enjoys, welcomes, & remains fastened to form, there arises delight.
sn22.5:3.4: Any delight in form is clinging.
sn22.5:3.5: From clinging/sustenance as a requisite condition comes becoming.
sn22.5:3.6: From becoming as a requisite condition comes birth.
sn22.5:3.7: From birth as a requisite condition, then aging & death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, & despair come into play.
sn22.5:3.8: Such is the origination of this entire mass of stress & suffering.
sn22.5:4.1: One enjoys, welcomes, & remains fastened to feeling…
sn22.5:4.4: One enjoys, welcomes, & remains fastened to consciousness.
sn22.5:4.5: As one enjoys, welcomes, & remains fastened to consciousness, there arises delight.
sn22.5:4.6: Any delight in consciousness is clinging.
sn22.5:4.7: From clinging/sustenance as a requisite condition comes becoming.
sn22.5:4.8: From becoming as a requisite condition comes birth.
sn22.5:4.9: From birth as a requisite condition, then aging & death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, & despair come into play.
sn22.5:4.10: Such is the origination of this entire mass of stress & suffering.
sn22.5:5.1: This, monks, is the origination of form.
sn22.5:6.1: And what is the disappearance of form… feeling…
sn22.5:6.4: What is the disappearance of consciousness?
sn22.5:7.1: There is the case where one doesn’t enjoy, welcome, or remain fastened.
sn22.5:8.1: And what does one not enjoy or welcome, to what does one not remain fastened?
sn22.5:8.2: One doesn’t enjoy, welcome, or remain fastened to form.
sn22.5:8.3: As one doesn’t enjoy, welcome, or remain fastened to form, any delight in form ceases.
sn22.5:8.4: From the cessation of delight comes the cessation of clinging.
sn22.5:8.5: From the cessation of clinging/sustenance, the cessation of becoming. From the cessation of becoming, the cessation of birth. From the cessation of birth, then aging & death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, & despair all cease.
sn22.5:8.6: Such is the cessation of this entire mass of stress & suffering.
sn22.5:9.1: One doesn’t enjoy, welcome, or remain fastened to feeling…
sn22.5:9.2: As one doesn’t enjoy, welcome, or remain fastened to feeling, any delight in feeling ceases.
sn22.5:9.3: From the cessation of delight comes the cessation of clinging.
sn22.5:9.4: From the cessation of clinging/sustenance, the cessation of becoming. From the cessation of becoming, the cessation of birth. From the cessation of birth, then aging & death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, & despair all cease.
sn22.5:9.5: Such is the cessation of this entire mass of stress & suffering.
sn22.5:11.1: One doesn’t enjoy, welcome, or remain fastened to consciousness.
sn22.5:11.2: As one doesn’t enjoy, welcome, or remain fastened to consciousness, any delight in consciousness ceases.
sn22.5:11.3: From the cessation of delight comes the cessation of clinging. From the cessation of clinging/sustenance, the cessation of becoming. From the cessation of becoming, the cessation of birth. From the cessation of birth, then aging & death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, & despair all cease.
sn22.5:11.4: Such is the cessation of this entire mass of stress & suffering.
sn22.5:12.1: This, monks, is the disappearance of form. This, the disappearance of feeling… perception… fabrications. This, the disappearance of consciousness.”
